text: A good day to you, my name is Shaoxiong. This is part one on a series of how to perform laparoscopic conventional and D-tree right hemicolectomy. I will not be talking about the literature behind the rationale of performing a D-tree right hemicolectomy as the purpose of the video is to detail the technical aspects of the surgery itself. In part one, I'll be going through the various approaches to performing a right hemicolectomy and how to perform right hemicolon mobilization. This is sufficient to perform a conventional right hemicolectomy. In part two, I'll be going into detail of the relevant lymphovascular anatomy, how to perform the central nodal dissection, as well as final specimen evaluation. Finally, in part three, I will comment on a summary operation.

In Singapore, D-tree right hemicolectomy is not yet widely practised. However, I believe that it is only a matter of time before it becomes standard of care. I would like to emphasise that although the videos concentrate on the technical details of surgery, case selection and perioperative management is just as, or even more important than doing the surgery itself. The surgery of cancer is a surgery of lymphatics. Here I will define what vessels are required to be taken for a right hemicolectomy. I refer to this article written by Professor Choi and his team in Kyungpook National University Hospital, South Korea. For cancers of the cecum, I would take the iliocollic artery, the right collic artery if present, and the right branch of middle collic artery. For an ascending colon tumour, the iliocolic artery and the right colic artery, if present, should be taken. In addition, the middle colic artery should also be taken. For hepatic flexure and transverse colon tumours, the iliocolic artery is not so important to be taken. However, the right colic artery, if present, and the middle colic artery should be taken.

The surgeon demonstrates the vessel-first medial-to-lateral approach for a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy. The ileocolic pedicle is lifted, and an incision is made below it to develop a plane. The artery and vein are identified, ligated, and divided. The mesentery is lifted, and medial-to-lateral mobilization is performed using blunt and sharp dissection until reaching the bowel laterally and superiorly. The duodenum is separated from the mesentery, and lateral attachments are taken down to complete the dissection.

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position with the small bowel flipped to the left upper quadrant. The assistant lifts the cecum, and the surgeon makes an inferior incision to separate the bowel from the retroperitoneum. The dissection plane is developed cranially, facilitating the mobilisation of the right hemicolon while avoiding injury to the gonadal vessels and ureter.

The surgeon initiates the supracollic approach to right hemicolon mobilization by placing the patient in a reverse Trendelenburg position. The lesser sac is accessed through an incision in the lesser omentum, and the gastroepiploic vessels are traced to the base of the transverse colon mesentery. A dissection plane is developed along the superior border of the transverse colon, facilitating separation from the duodenum, hepatic flexure takedown, and lateral to medial mobilization of the upper right hemicolon. The gastrocolic trunk vessels are visible, and the colic branch of the trunk of Henle is divided, allowing the transverse colon to be flipped down. Further dissection exposes the C-loop of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas. The patient is then repositioned in the Trendelenburg position to complete mobilization through an inferior approach.

The surgeon performs the supracolic approach to right hemicolon mobilization with the patient in a reverse Trendelenburg position. The assistant holds the stomach or gastroepiploic vessels, while the surgeon enters the lesser sac by incising the omentum below the gastroepiploic vessels. Dissection follows the gastroepiploic vessels towards the pancreas, dividing small branches to the colon. A bloodless plane is found between the duodenum and transverse colon mesentery, leading to the hepatic flexure. Careful isolation and division of the registrar's vein from the gastrocolic trunk of Henle exposes the head of the pancreas. The hepatic flexure is taken down, and dissection continues until the third part of the duodenum is exposed. The patient is then placed in the Trendelenburg position to complete right hemicolon mobilization inferiorly.
